question_title: Will Delhi's Safdarjung station record a maximum temperature of 45°C or higher on any day between May 25 and May 31, 2026?
summary: The probability of Delhi Safdarjung recording ≥45°C during May 25-31 is estimated at 55%, reflecting a tense disagreement between authoritative sources. The IMD's own official forecast bulletin explicitly shows 44-46°C for Safdarjung for May 25-28, with heat wave conditions expected — strongly favoring YES. However, three independent NWP models (ECMWF, ICON, Best Match) consistently show 42-43°C, well below the threshold. The effective window is only 4 days (May 25-28) due to an agreed-upon sharp cooling May 29-30. Monte Carlo simulations with blended model centers (43.5-44.5°C) and 1.5°C forecast uncertainty give 42-73% depending on center assumptions. The final estimate of 55% weights the IMD's local expertise and specific Safdarjung forecast substantially while acknowledging the strong NWP model consensus pointing lower. Safdarjung's tendency to run 1-2°C below other Delhi stations adds further downside risk.

factors.description: IMD official forecast bulletin (May 22, 2030 IST) shows 44-46°C for Safdarjung specifically for May 25-28, verified by matching May 22 observed value (43.3°C) in the same forecast table. The midpoint of this range is 45°C, directly at the threshold. IMD forecasters see NWP model outputs and still chose to forecast higher.

IMD forecasts 'heat wave conditions very likely during next 6 days' with orange alert through May 27. However, heat wave can be declared at departure ≥4.5°C (possible at 44.5°C), not only at ≥45°C absolute. Mildly supportive but less direct than the temperature range.

Three independent NWP models (ECMWF: 42.6-42.8°C, ICON: 41.9-42.7°C, Best Match: 42.8-43.2°C) consistently show May 25-28 maxima at 42-43°C, well below the 45°C threshold. Strong counter-signal to IMD's forecast.

Historical base rate is only ~10-18% for this window. Reaching 45°C requires departure of +4.7°C from normal — significantly more than the +3.1°C currently observed. This is near-heatwave territory that only occurs in exceptional years.
El Niño developing (82% probability per NOAA), below-normal monsoon forecast (92% LPA), and no monsoon relief for Delhi until late June favor prolonged, intensified heat.

Safdarjung already recorded 44.6°C on May 7, 2026, demonstrating the station can approach 45°C this season. Current departure +3.1°C with IMD expecting further intensification (rise ~1°C after May 23, min temps rising 3°C by May 25).

All models agree on sharp temperature drop May 29-30 (~35-39°C) due to pre-monsoon weather disturbance. This effectively eliminates May 29-31 from the window, leaving only 4 days (May 25-28) for a potential 45°C reading.

Safdarjung consistently runs 1-2°C cooler than other Delhi stations. Ridge hit 45.3°C on May 21 while Safdarjung was only 43.6°C (1.7°C gap). Even if Delhi broadly hits 46°C, Safdarjung may only reach 44-45°C.

reasoning:
**My forecast: 55% probability (YES)**

## Key Analysis

**Resolution criteria:** Safdarjung station (IMD 42182) must record ≥45.0°C on any day May 25-31, 2026 per IMD data.

**The central tension is between two authoritative sources:**

| Source | Forecast for May 25-28 | Implied P(≥45°C) |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **IMD official forecast** | 44-46°C (Safdarjung-specific) | ~60-75% |
| **ECMWF/ICON/Best Match** | 42-43°C | ~10-25% |
| **GFS** (known warm bias) | 46-47°C | ~95%+ |

**Why this is genuinely close to a coin flip:**
- ✅ The IMD's forecast table is verified as Safdarjung-specific (May 22 observed = 43.3°C matches). Their 44-46°C range includes 45°C+ outcomes. IMD forecasters see NWP outputs and still forecast higher.
- ❌ Three independent NWP models agree on 42-43°C — a full 2°C below threshold. Historical base rate is only ~15%. Safdarjung runs 1-2°C cooler than other Delhi stations.
- ⏰ The effective window is only 4 days (May 25-28), as all models agree on sharp cooling May 29-30.

The Monte Carlo simulation with blended center (~44.0-44.2°C) and 1.5°C forecast uncertainty gives ~55-64% probability across reasonable assumptions.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Delhi's Safdarjung station record a maximum temperature of 45°C or higher on any day between May 25 and May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43572)

[Delhi Heatwave 2026: National Capital Records Warmest May Night in 14 Years as IMD Issues Orange Alert Till May 27](https://sundayguardianlive.com/india/delhi-heatwave-2026-national-capital-record-warmest-may-night-in-14-years-as-imd-issues-orange-alert-till-may-27-delhi-weather-195148/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, on any calendar day from May 25, 2026 through May 31, 2026 (inclusive, India Standard Time), the maximum temperature recorded at the New Delhi – Safdarjung observatory (IMD station 42182) is at least 45.0°C as reported by the India Meteorological Department. The maximum temperature can be checked on IMD's city weather page for Safdarjung (https://city.imd.gov.in/citywx/city_weather.php?id=42182) or in IMD's daily "All India Weather Summary" bulletins published at https://mausam.imd.gov.in. If the Safdarjung observation for any day within this window shows a max of 45.0°C or higher per IMD, the question resolves YES; otherwise NO. If IMD's data for Safdarjung is unavailable for the entire window, resolution will rely on figures reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) explicitly citing IMD's Safdarjung observation.
fine_print: Only the Safdarjung station counts; readings from other Delhi stations (e.g., Palam, Ayanagar, Ridge, Lodi Road) do not trigger resolution even if they exceed 45°C. If IMD revises an initially reported value, the final/revised IMD value governs.

factors.description: IMD official forecast shows 44-46°C for Safdarjung specifically for May 25-28 (verified by matching May 22 observed value of 43.3°C in same table). This range includes 45°C+ outcomes.
IMD explicitly forecasts 'heat wave conditions very likely during next 6 days' and 'HEAT WAVE CONDITIONS AT A FEW PLACES' for May 23-28. Heat wave by actual max temp is defined as ≥45°C.
ECMWF, ICON, and Best Match NWP models all show 42-43°C for May 25-28, well below 45°C threshold. Three independent models agree.
Historical base rate: Only ~10-18% chance of ≥45°C at Safdarjung during May 25-31 in current climate (1 out of 11 years 2015-2025)
El Niño developing (82% probability) and below-normal monsoon forecast favor prolonged and intensified heat, no monsoon relief for Delhi until late June
Safdarjung already recorded 44.6°C on May 7, 2026, showing the station CAN approach 45°C this season. Current departure +3.1°C with expected intensification
Sharp temperature drop expected May 29-30 (all models agree: ~35-39°C) due to pre-monsoon weather disturbance - effectively eliminates May 29-31 from the window
Safdarjung consistently runs 1-2°C cooler than other Delhi stations (Ridge, Mungeshpur). Ridge hit 45.3°C on May 21 while Safdarjung was only 43.6°C
News reports state 'Delhi boils at 46°C' and minimum temp hit 31.9°C (warmest May night in 14 years), indicating exceptional heat conditions

input.anchor: Historical base rate: ~15% (1 out of 11 years 2015-2025 had Safdarjung ≥45°C during May 25-31; current climate estimate 10-18%). This is the unconditional climatological probability without forecast information.
input.assessment:
This question hinges on whether Delhi Safdarjung will reach an extreme but not unprecedented temperature threshold during the climatologically hottest week of the year. 

PRO (YES): IMD's own official forecast bulletin specifically for Safdarjung shows 44-46°C for May 25-28. IMD forecasters have local expertise and see the NWP model outputs before issuing their forecast - they chose to forecast higher than models. Heat wave conditions are officially expected. El Niño conditions and record warm nights suggest exceptional heat. Safdarjung already hit 44.6°C this season.

CON (NO): Three independent NWP models (ECMWF, ICON, Best Match) consistently show 42-43°C, well below 45°C. The historical base rate is only 10-18%. Safdarjung runs cooler than other Delhi stations by 1-2°C. The sharp cooling May 29-30 eliminates 3 of the 7 days. NWP models are calibrated and have good track records.

The key tension: IMD forecasters vs. NWP models. IMD has local expertise but sometimes overpredicts in ranges. NWP models might underpredict urban heat extremes. My compromise weights IMD's forecast significantly (they are the domain experts issuing this specific forecast) while acknowledging the models suggest lower temperatures.

Monte Carlo simulation with center 44.0-44.5°C and sigma 1.5°C gives ~58-73% for May 25-28. Blending across model uncertainty scenarios gives ~59%.
input.calibration_notes: The anchor base rate of 15% gets a large update from the IMD's specific forecast of 44-46°C. Moving from 15% to 59% represents a significant update justified by the IMD's explicit forecast. The NWP model disagreement keeps me from going higher. Status quo would be NO (most years don't reach 45°C), but the active heatwave forecast shifts this.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether IMD's 44-46°C range represents the central forecast for Safdarjung or a broader range for Delhi. 2. Whether NWP models or IMD forecasters are more accurate for extreme heat events. 3. Whether the May 29-30 cooling event will arrive as forecast or be delayed.
input.tool_audit: research() tool was highly effective - returned multi-model forecast data, historical statistics, and current conditions. IMD PDF was successfully fetched and contained critical official forecast data. Web search found relevant news articles confirming the heatwave context. IMD city weather page (city.imd.gov.in) failed to load actual data, but the PDF bulletin was much more informative. Open-Meteo API data from research agent provided precise NWP model forecasts.
input.update_triggers: 1. May 23-24 observed temperatures at Safdarjung - if they reach 45°C or exceed the IMD forecast range, P(YES) increases significantly. 2. If the May 29-30 cooling arrives early (May 28) or late (May 31). 3. Updated IMD or NWP model forecasts in coming days.
input.process_reflection: The research() tool worked excellently here, pulling multi-model NWP data and historical statistics in a single call. The IMD PDF fetch was critical - it provided the official forecast table that is the single most important piece of evidence. The main friction was uncertainty about how to interpret the IMD's range forecast (44-46°C) - whether it's a confidence interval or a min-max. The Monte Carlo simulation approach worked well for this threshold question, though the results are quite sensitive to the assumed center temperature. A tool that could pull IMD station-specific forecasts directly would be valuable.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Summary

I reviewed the full research trace, all 9 factors, the Monte Carlo simulations, worldview research entries, and the adversarial self-examination. No worldview forecasts or prior session forecasts exist for this question (43572), so no cross-question consistency issues arise.

## Factor-by-factor verification

**Factor 1 (IMD forecast 44-46°C, logit=+1.5):** ✓ Grounded. The trace shows the agent read the IMD Delhi forecast PDF (dated May 22, 2026, 2030 IST). The agent's thinking section confirms it saw the 7-day forecast table showing 44-46°C for May 24-28 and verified the May 22 observed value was 43.3°C in the same table, confirming it is Safdarjung-specific. This is the dominant factor and is well-supported.

**Factor 2 (Heat wave = ≥45°C, logit=+1.0):** ⚠️ Overstated inference. The factor claims "Heat wave by actual max temp is defined as ≥45°C." But IMD's own definition (confirmed in worldview research at delhi-heatwave-may-2026-imd-forecast-temperature-o-17153757.json line 53) is: "max temp ≥ 45°C **or** departure from normal ≥ 4.5°C." The "or" is critical — IMD can declare a heatwave at Safdarjung even with temperatures of only 44.5°C (40°C normal + 4.5°C departure). Therefore, the heat wave warning does NOT necessarily imply IMD expects ≥45°C; it could be triggered by the departure-based threshold. The factor's implied reasoning (heat wave warning → they expect ≥45°C) is an overstatement. However, since Factor 1 already carries the stronger signal from the actual temperature range forecast, this overstatement doesn't dramatically change the overall picture.

**Factor 3 (NWP models 42-43°C, logit=-1.5):** ✓ Grounded. Trace confirms ECMWF: 42.8/42.6/42.6/42.1°C; ICON: 41.9/42.0/42.7/42.7°C; Best Match: 42.8/43.1/43.2/42.0°C for May 25-28.

**Factor 4 (Historical base rate ~10-18%, logit=-1.0):** ✓ Grounded. Worldview research confirms 1 out of 11 years (2024 only) reached 45°C during May 25-31 in 2015-2025.

**Factors 5-9:** All verified against trace sources. Factor 6's 44.6°C on May 7 is from the Metaculus question background. Factor 8's Ridge vs Safdarjung gap (45.3°C vs 43.6°C on May 21) is from the IMD PDF. Factor 9's 46°C headline was confirmed as NOT specifically attributed to Safdarjung by the fetched article, but the factor correctly uses it only as context for "exceptional heat conditions."

## Double-counting concern

**Factors 1 and 2** draw on the same IMD bulletin — Factor 1 cites the temperature range forecast, and Factor 2 cites the heat wave warning from the same document. These are not fully independent pieces of evidence; the heat wave warning and the 44-46°C forecast are products of the same forecasting process. This represents mild double-counting, inflating the positive signal by ~0.7 effective logits.

## Probability assessment

The agent arrives at 59%. The key tension — IMD forecast (44-46°C center ~45°C) vs. three independent NWP models (42-43°C consensus) — is real and well-identified. The agent's Monte Carlo framework shows sensitivity to the assumed center: 43.5°C → 42%, 44.0°C → 58%, 44.5°C → 73%.

I believe the agent's weighting is slightly generous toward the IMD forecast. Three independent NWP models agreeing on 42-43°C is strong signal. The agent's own adversarial counterargument raises the valid point that IMD's 44-46°C range may represent a generous bounding box rather than a point forecast, and that reaching 45°C requires a departure of +4.7°C from normal — significantly more than the +3.1°C already observed. A probability around 40-50% would better reflect the tension between IMD and NWP consensus, and the Factor 2 overstatement contributes to the upward pull.

However, the agent's 59% is not so far from the plausible range that it constitutes a clear error. The IMD's forecast IS specifically for Safdarjung (verified by the observed value match), and IMD forecasters do incorporate local knowledge beyond NWP output. The difference between my ~45% and the agent's 59% is meaningful (~14pp) but within the range of reasonable disagreement about how to weight domain expertise vs. model consensus.

## Other checks

- **Resolution criteria:** Present, well-engaged. The agent correctly focuses on Safdarjung-only, IMD as authority, and the May 25-31 window. ✓
- **Wrong-direction factors:** None found. ✓
- **Contradictory assessment:** The narrative is consistent with the factors. ✓
- **Counterargument quality:** Strong — constructs a coherent NWP-dominant case with specific evidence. ✓
- **Confidence in estimate (0.40):** Appropriately low given the large model disagreement. ✓
- **Tool audit:** Consistent with ground truth metrics. ✓
- **Anchor divergence:** Anchor is 15%, final is 59% — a 44pp departure. The dominant factor (IMD forecast, effective logit +1.2) is strong but the departure is large. Given the double-counting of Factors 1+2, removing Factor 2 would reduce the factor sum from 0.71 to ~0.01, which would imply ~50% — still a large departure from 15% but more reasonable. The anchor departure is mostly justified by the IMD forecast.

## Verdict: warn

The Factor 2 overstatement (equating "heat wave" with ≥45°C when IMD's definition also includes a departure-based threshold that can trigger at 44.5°C) represents a meaningful analytical error that inflates the positive signal, and it partially double-counts with Factor 1 (same IMD bulletin). The probability of 59% is somewhat high relative to the NWP consensus, though not dramatically wrong. These issues collectively warrant a warn rather than a fail, as the core direction of the forecast (above base rate, reflecting IMD's explicit forecast) is well-supported.
